does: This paper introduces MemoAttack, a black-box jailbreak framework that systematically organizes and evolves attack experience into reusable skill-structured memory. The core contribution is moving beyond sample-wise heuristic search to build a persistent, organized attack memory where individual units pair attack skills with templates, evidence, and lifecycle states. The framework includes three main components: skill-structured memory modeling that abstracts attack patterns into reusable units, lifecycle-driven memory evolution that updates units based on evidence of success or failure, and principled memory selection mechanisms for generating new attacks. The method demonstrates improved efficiency and effectiveness in finding adversarial prompts that trigger harmful outputs from LLMs, evaluated on diverse safety-relevant attack scenarios.
connects: This work builds on a growing line of research in automated jailbreak generation and adversarial attacks against LLMs. It extends prior black-box attack methods that either rely on greedy heuristic search or maintain unstructured strategy pools by introducing systematic knowledge management analogous to evolutionary algorithms and reinforcement learning frameworks. The skill-structured memory concept draws inspiration from program synthesis and meta-learning approaches that organize and reuse learned behaviors. The paper engages with safety evaluation literature that emphasizes the importance of finding diverse failure modes, and responds to limitations of existing attack libraries and strategy repositories that lack principled organization, learning mechanisms, and lifecycle management for accumulated attack knowledge.
enables: This framework opens directions for more efficient and systematic adversarial evaluation of LLM safety. The structured memory approach could enable transfer of attack skills across different models and domains, reducing the sample complexity of discovering jailbreaks. Future work could investigate how skill-structured memories generalize to new LLMs or defensive mechanisms, and whether memory insights reveal fundamental vulnerabilities in alignment approaches. The lifecycle-driven evolution mechanism could be extended to incorporate human feedback or red-teaming data, creating collaborative human-AI attack frameworks. Additionally, understanding what makes certain attack skills reusable and transferable could inform better defenses and provide insights into LLM robustness properties across different architectural choices and training objectives.
